stations.label: THE FIRST SILVER PENNY
stations.line: On the occasion of my beginning, I was struck in silver by an Anglo-Saxon king — a single pound of it weighed out into two hundred and forty pieces, and the arithmetic of that first day has never entirely let me go.
stations.era_photo: There can be no photograph — instead render as a period-appropriate visual: a hand-illuminated manuscript portrait on vellum, a robed regal figure in the Mercian court of Offa, gold leaf and mineral pigment, cracked with age, a scattering of hammered silver pennies laid before her.

stations.label: THE FIRST GOLD SOVEREIGN
stations.line: In recognition of a Tudor's ambition, I was for the first time rendered in gold and made to bear the sovereign's likeness enthroned — an image, you understand, chosen not for beauty but for the plain assertion that the coin and the Crown were one and the same thing.
stations.era_photo: Impossible as photograph — render as a Tudor court oil portrait in the manner of the period: a poised figure in rich brocade and ermine before a dark ground, a large gold sovereign held to the light, warm varnished tones, the flat formal stare of early modern portraiture.

stations.label: THE BANK IS FOUNDED
stations.line: It gives me great pleasure to record that in this year a Bank was raised in my name to lend a war-strained king one million two hundred thousand pounds — from that day I was no longer merely metal, but a promise institutions agreed to keep, which is a far more durable form of being.
stations.era_photo: Impossible as photograph — render as a late-seventeenth-century engraved plate: a stately woman in a mantua gown and lace, standing within the colonnade of the new Bank of England, fine cross-hatched linework, sepia ink on laid paper.

stations.label: THE GOLD STANDARD ADOPTED
stations.line: The Great Recoinage bound me formally to gold, and I confess I rather enjoyed that century of certainty — every note I bore was a solemn undertaking that I could be exchanged for a fixed weight of the stuff, and the world, for a time, believed us both.
stations.era_photo: A very early daguerreotype, 1840s process applied retroactively — a Regency-into-Victorian woman in a high-waisted gown and bonnet, seated stiffly beside a strongbox, silvered mirror surface, long exposure blur at the hands, tarnished plate edges.

stations.label: LEAVING THE GOLD STANDARD
stations.line: In the autumn of that year I was obliged to release my hold on gold entirely, and I would ask you to note that I did so without raising my voice — a lady does not announce the end of an empire's certainty, she simply carries on, and I carried on.
stations.era_photo: A 1931 silver gelatin press photograph, high contrast — a composed woman in a drop-waist day dress and cloche hat outside the Bank of England amid a crowd of homburg-hatted men, grainy monochrome, slight motion blur, newspaper halftone dots.

stations.label: DECIMAL DAY
stations.line: On the fifteenth of February I set aside twelve hundred years of shillings and pence for a tidy arithmetic of hundreds — a modernisation I found sensible, though I shall admit privately that I missed the old counting, which had a music to it that decimals rather lack.
stations.era_photo: A 1971 Kodachrome colour photograph — an elegant woman in a tailored wool coat and structured handbag at a shop counter, new decimal coins and conversion charts on the counter, saturated period colour, soft grain, that particular warm Kodachrome red.

stations.label: BLACK WEDNESDAY
stations.line: On the sixteenth of September I was forced from the Exchange Rate Mechanism in a single afternoon, and while it was, I grant you, a most undignified departure, I would gently observe that surviving one's humiliations intact is rather the entire art of endurance.
stations.era_photo: A 1992 35mm colour news photograph — a poised woman in a navy skirt suit before a wall of falling green trading screens, dealers in shirtsleeves behind her, slightly cool flash-lit tone, the specific flat look of early-nineties photojournalism.

stations.label: THE KING'S NEW NOTES
stations.line: It is my present occasion to appear bearing a new sovereign's likeness upon my notes, the first change of face in seventy years, and I find — as I always have — that continuity is not the absence of change but the grace with which one accommodates it.
stations.era_photo: A 2024 high-resolution digital photograph — a serene woman in an impeccable pale coat and single strand of pearls, holding a crisp new polymer banknote bearing a fresh royal portrait, clean natural window light, sharp full-frame detail, subtle depth of field.
